sentences.text: We didn't have too much time to say-- everything was [NON-ENGLISH].
And if you didn't [NON-ENGLISH], they hit you in the head.
Everything was fast, fast, fast.
No talk.
No, none whatsoever.
What I know of is a sister who went to work, [?
Hansa, ?] Hannah and a brother [NON-ENGLISH], older than me by two years, and a brother [NON-ENGLISH] that ran away from the ghetto.
And not-- not-- right now, I mean, nobody survived.
None of them.
Yeah, my brother in the camp, and I know that he escaped again from a train that another couple of young men, I was told.
And they stopped the train.
It was an open train.
And they stopped the train.
That's already in-- to the end of the war, only a month or two months before the ending of the war.
And ran down from the train in a wooded area.
And they stopped the train.
And they went after them with the dogs.
And they shot them.
No, no.
Everything was fast, fast, fast.
No, no.
The camp, from Auschwitz?
There were barracks.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I would say rows and rows of barracks.
Inside the barracks, there was [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] 10 feet, or eight feet leeway.
And then they had where to sleep.
And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] how should I describe it?
Wooden things, a little hay on top of the wood.
And that's the way, you just went to-- went to sleep there.
That's what I remember.
No, there were three layers, three layers, one, two, three, in Auschwitz.
In Auschwitz camp, I remember three layers.
And after the trip from the-- from he train, the smell-- and I had a shower.
And it felt nothing better.
I could have slept on cement, I mean wood or something, just the shower, slept, slept over.
And early in the morning, they got us up.
What?
No, no, no.
They had-- outside, they had an area which you have a bathroom to go, not in the same place.
No bath-- washing, forget about it, no washing.
Eating, they gave in the morning.
They gave coffee, black coffee, with a piece of bread.
Huh?
Under, [INAUDIBLE] lying [INAUDIBLE].
No, no, in the barracks, outside the barracks in Auschwitz.
We cleaned, cleaned the floor, cleaned the floors, I mean, outside, picked up papers, swept.
That's all what I did.
Not in Auschwitz, that I know of, not in Auschwitz.
In the other camp I went to, I met some rabbi.
No, no, no.
No, didn't see them anymore.
And we were talking to the other people.
And there were rumors, rumors.
And maybe-- I couldn't-- they were talking about the horrible things what happened.
It didn't penetrate my head, things like this.
I always hoped this is going to get over.
And my hope, I hoped, and my belief in Hashem kept me going.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] otherwise, I would have been finished many times.
Whatever I could, not-- not [INAUDIBLE].
I didn't have it.
They took everything away in the wagon.
When we came to Auschwitz, we had [INAUDIBLE].
Nothing, even my underwear when I took a shower.
I remember after I was selected for work, I took a shower.
And I had my mother sew money, Hungarian money, in my underwear, 500 pengos.
And I was holding on to it.
Like, it's my underwear.
I'm going to use it.
And a capo came over, and gave me a flop.
I shall get into the shower, and grabbed it away, and just threw it.
He didn't know--
No, no.
Just whatever was yours, they took.
Everything took away, only thing-- nothing.
They just gave me the striped uniform, and the-- oh, the cap, and that's it.
And shoes, what I had.
They left me the shoes what I came in.
Nothing to select by age.
Had nothing to do with age.
The way he saw-- they didn't take any young people under 15 or 14.
If slipped through if the kid was husky and tall, and if he told he's 16 or 18, they let him go ahead.
But if a kid was 16 and he looked 12, he went to the gas chamber.
The older people went to the gas chambers and the crematorium.
A couple of days, a couple, two, or three days.
After the two or three days, they, like I mentioned before, they counted certain amount of people.
And they put me in-- put us in a train wagons.
And we went for a couple of days overnight.
And we arrived in a place called Dyhernfurth, in a camp.
That was a labor camp.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] With electric fences, on top, they had machine guns around the camp.
And they had factories not far away from the camps.
And we arrived there in afternoon.
And next day, we went to work.
Put us in a barracks.
The barracks contained of double beds.
We got a blanket, and two beds [INAUDIBLE].
We were in a barrack, I don't know how many people.
I would say 150, 200, I don't know, a lot of people.
And kept spotless, clean.
And--
We-- we had to.
After we came home from work, or in the morning when we got up before, we had to make the beds.
And it had to be just so.
In Dyhernfurth, nobody.
Only with me a friend of mine.
His name was [NON-ENGLISH] from the town.
We Grew up together.
I would say 95% was Jewish people in that camp.
There was some Poles, and some German with a green thing.
What I understand, they took out the criminals from the-- I as told, from the prisons.
And they made them the leaders of us in the barracks.
In every barrack, they had a-- a-- a blockeldester.
And somehow, I don't know, he didn't go to work.
And he took care in the camp.
And he distribute the food [INAUDIBLE] for us.
And so, therefore, I would say 95% Jewish people.
In that camp, no.
I met people from different-- what we talked.
But they came from two towns further, or a town closer, or from Czechoslovakia, or from Hungary.
I met in the camp people from Poland-- Jewish people, not really communicate in English.
I went to work there, yes, on we called it a [NON-ENGLISH].
Went to work-- early in the morning, they put us on the-- on the-- on the appellplatz, and the counting, and counting, count again, and the numbers.
And they yelled out so many people, so many people, so many people.
And the Gestapos to the bigger officer, smaller officer, bigger officer, then so on.
And they write it down.
And then we have to staying in the appellplatz an hour, two hours, morning and night.
And then we marched out to work with the machine guns.
And we took along a couple of wagons, small wagons, for pulling along.
A lot of people died on the work.
A lot of people just got sick on the work.
And then we brought them back at night in that wagon when we finished working.
No, no--
They had it organized.
It was organized from the guards from the camp, the obersturmfuhrer, the commander, from the camp.
And we went to-- went to work.
And they were all falling.
Well, the food was very, very, very little, very little.
They gave in the evening, one-- they gave a piece of bread cut.
They cut a bread in six portions, six or eight I'm not sure.
And then they gave a soup.
It was a soup, a potato soup, or another soup, I don't know.
A soup and a piece of bread in the evening.
In the morning, it was only coffee, black coffee.
That's it.
That was the ration.
And therefore, middle aged people fell.
Older people fell on the work, very hard work.
And therefore, we had the wagons.
And we just threw them on the wagon.
And we schlepped them in, brought them into the camp.
[INAUDIBLE] took them away.
And the others at that camp, I was on the appellplatz.
And I met [NON-ENGLISH], the rabbi, if I remember this, I think a rabbi.
That camp, I met [INAUDIBLE].
I was just-- it so happened I stayed on the appellplatz.
And he was next to me.
And he said, in the evening it was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think it was [INAUDIBLE] said the Kaddish.
And it was quiet, very quiet, [INAUDIBLE] in the morning.
So I kept-- always, somehow, I winded up in that line [INAUDIBLE].
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I believe he survived.
In the camp.
What?
I never saw him after the war.
I know of him.
I beg your pardon?
Well, that gave me [INAUDIBLE].
In that camp, I saw-- I learned one thing in that camp.
The person what had hopes and belief better survived than the atheist.
The atheist had no for tomorrow and didn't see any tomorrow.
[INAUDIBLE] finished.
And they talked against existing and the God and so on.
And a lot of them-- some of them went to took their life.
Some of them hang themselves.
Some of them went to the electric fence.
The people what believed there is a tomorrow, and you have to survive and tell the story.
I was told many times, let's not forget, and tell what's going on here, if anybody survives.
[SOB] We kept-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we kept going.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] I think that it helped to survive, anybody believed in Hashem for tomorrow.
And we said, we hope for tomorrow.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And then after a while, in that camp I spent the whole summer, practically.
And I became a Muselmann.
I was--
A Muselmann is very skinny.
I lost-- I became only bones.
Bones is called a Muselmann.
And they used to make selections once in awhile on the appellplatz.
When they became very skinny and very weak, they didn't send them out to work.
They just took them away, and send them away to die, or the gas chamber, or I don't know where.
They just-- they had no use for that bodies, for that people anymore.
I became very, very weak, also my friend.
[INAUDIBLE], he was older than me a little bit, two years older, I think, two or three years, two years.
We had a man by the name [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Fishov in that camp.
And Fishov had a son, Moishe Fishov, my age.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It was already in late July, August or July, in the summer time.
And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] his son got also very weak.
And he was a tall man.
And he was also becoming-- getting there.
People were falling every day.
People were dying in the camp, and at work.
He went over to the obersturmfuhrer in the evening somehow.
He had a courage.
He could have gotten killed.
And he had a discussion.
He spoke-- very intelligent man, that Fishov.
He spoke a very perfect German, excellent German.
And-- and the man was an old man, the Gestapo, obersturmfuhrer.
I can see him today with white hair.
And he told them that you have young people here, like my age.
You've got to-- and you're killing their purpose.
You're killing them.
And the Reich is losing out.
The German people are losing out.
Instead, you should teach them a trade, and put them to work, and make something out of them.
And they'll give you many years to existing.
And, somehow, it penetrated on Hashem.
Somehow, he listened to Hashem.
He was a religious man, [INAUDIBLE].
And the next morning, we went on the appellplatz, and not [INAUDIBLE] look at the papers, or the age, or asking.
They just went through us, you know, the Gestapo, and looked at the faces.
And I was one of them picked out to learn a trade.
And his son, Moishe, was also picked out.
We went, and they opened up a barrack, a special barrack.
And they brought machinery to teach us a trade [NON-ENGLISH].
Locksmith.
So that was a lifesaver for a couple of weeks, about six weeks, I don't know.
I don't remember.
What was the lifesaving?
Number one, we got better food from the kitchen.
The kitchen was right there.
No, we were not working.
I mean, we were students.
We became students overnight to teach us a trade as--
Locksmiths
Better food, and better conditions.
And we didn't work so hard.
And we had time to-- for, you know, [INAUDIBLE] the life changed that morning.
And I came back to myself.
You know, instead I should be only bones.
It came back, the meat on me, you know?
And so [INAUDIBLE] Moishe Fishov, was pretty good.
I even remember that [INAUDIBLE] and we had to sing [SPEAKING GERMAN], and so on, you know?
The way they taught us.
That was one thing in this conservation camp was a good thing for, as far as I was concerned, a lifesaver.
They taught me-- what?
They taught you the German words?
Yeah, they taught me the German words, and to speak German, and to speak-- the name of the tool, or the machinery, or the tools.
I learned it.
I learned that time, in that period, I learned German, from that school.
That was like a school, what we created in the camp on account of this Fishov.
Oh, we were 25 to 30, young fellas, all in the age of 14 to 18, all in that age.
And then I remember another thing in the camp.
In the evening, I was walking.
And the Gestapos used to walk in with his shiny boots, like who knows what, walking down.
And I walked-- you know, I went to the bathroom.
Of They had a bathroom not in the barracks, built a barrack in the bathroom.
And I went.
And I somehow-- and, you there?
One of the Gestapos called me over.
This is the only time I-- you there, come here.
Come here.
Come over.
The [NON-ENGLISH], the cap, was standing.
Instead, it should stay straight.
It stayed not right.
[INAUDIBLE].
And he showed his [INAUDIBLE] friend And he gave me a flop in the teeth.
I lost two teeth that time.
And blood was running.
I fell down the floor.
And they picked me up.
And they kept on walking.
OK, that was one incident, two incident-- two incidents in this camp for me.
Then was another incident-- not an incident.
It was a Jewish from Lodz, a Jewish man, his named Moishe.
And they used to come in, the Nazis, in the evening for entertainment, or hitting, or just having a good time, or whatever.
And this Moishe was a [HEBREW] They put him up on a pedestal to sing.
And I remember-- he had a voice, you could hear him for miles-- little guy, but tremendous voice.
And put him up on a platform.
And everybody was out from the barracks.
And he sung.
And we gathered around.
And he sung a Yiddish song.
And he sung a song [SINGING YIDDISH].
And he had more versions like that.
That's what I remember.
And then I remember a song he sung, [SPEAKING YIDDISH].
This incident, that was in that camp, that incident.
Came about August, end of August I would say, they evacuated the camp.
They evacuated the camp.
And we marched-- not march day and a night, not terrible, to [NON-ENGLISH].
Yeah, yeah, yeah.
You had-- you gave them-- you left and once on the route.
We got to Funfteichen, to a labor camp.
Funfteichen, they had the [GERMAN] factories.
[GERMAN].
[GERMAN].
[GERMAN].
You ever heard of [GERMAN] factories?
Even now, they're existing in Germany, [GERMAN].
The name of the factories in Germany.
They made machine-- tanks, guns.
They had factories all over Germany.
They had factories in the tunnels in the mountains.
And I-- this Funfteichen was [GERMAN] factories, I remember.
I don't know how many people we were there when we came there, put us in a barracks, on the same type of barracks.
There was in late August, August and September.
On Film was I came already in the fall and winter.
And we went out to the [INAUDIBLE] to work every day from morning till night.
I still had my friend Yankev Hirsch from the town of [NON-ENGLISH] with me.
And he became already-- he had swollen feet from water in the feet.
He got [INAUDIBLE] or whatever he developed.
And I was already in-- in that camp, we had Jewish blockeldester.
Instead of German, we had Jews as blockeldester.
And we had Polish-- I had a Pollock, Polish, blockeldester, kapos, and so on.
And there already in that camp, you had French people-- Jewish from France.
You had Polish.
You had Czechoslovakia.
You had from all over.
Jewish, yeah.
And we-- in December, 19-- end of December, 1944, I think, and beginning of January, it was very cold.
The order came that we gonna march-- close the camp.
And we marching.
